Title: Detoxification of hostplant’s chemical defence rather than its anti-predator co-option drives β-glucosidase-mediated lepidopteran counteradaptation

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: 30th generation inbred line derived from Utah population
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Used as a transgenic silencing target to deplete HGL-DTGs in plants fed to Manduca sexta larvae
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prior stable gene silencing line used in this study as Lyc4-depleted control; metabolite profiling of HGL-DTGs; genetic crossing with irBG1
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing GGPPS depletes the HGL-DTG precursor pool and lowers Lyc4/Nic1/Nic2 levels, enabling tests of Lyc4-dependent effects on larval physiology and predation.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Lyciumoside IV (Lyc4); nicotianoside I; nicotianoside II; HGL-DTGs
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GGPPS is described as the gene responsible for synthesis of the HGL-DTG precursor geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate. 2. The irGGPPS line provides a Lyc4-depleted plant background. 3. Absence of moulting defects in BG1-silenced larvae on irGGPPS-derived low-Lyc4 plants showed that toxicity depends on Lyc4 rather than BG1 silencing alone.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating GGPPS can modulate HGL-DTG-based defence levels in plants, but may broadly affect diterpenoid precursor supply.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although central for generating the Lyc4-depleted control, GGPPS is not the main experimental focus of the Results relative to BG1.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Deglucosylates lyciumoside IV (Lyc4) by removing the glucose moiety at C-17 to form 3-O-[α-rhamnopyranosyl-(1→4)-β-glucopyranosyl]-17-hydroxygeranyllinalool (RGHGL), thereby partially deglycosylating and detoxifying the compound.
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Xenobiotic detoxification of host-plant 17-hydroxygeranyllinalool diterpene glycosides (HGL-DTGs)
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Partial deglycosylation step of Lyc4 detoxification
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Ingestion of Lyc4 from Nicotiana attenuata foliage or artificial diet containing Lyc4
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced in larval midgut upon Lyc4 ingestion; also detected in foregut and hindgut, with strongest functional emphasis in midgut
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Plant-mediated RNAi silencing; qRT-PCR; in vitro enzyme assay with midgut extracts; inhibitor assay with D-gluconolactone; metabolite profiling in larval tissues and frass; dietary induction assay; complementation with Lyc4/RGHGL
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A promising pest-control target; silencing insect BG1 via plant-mediated RNAi can impair detoxification of HGL-DTGs and increase larval mortality under Lyc4-rich feeding conditions.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly detoxifies the target metabolite by converting it to a less toxic derivative
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: In larvae, unmetabolized Lyc4 accumulates mainly in midgut and hindgut; RGHGL is excreted in frass
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Midgut-expressed and Lyc4-inducible in feeding larvae
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing causes moulting impairment and mortality only when Lyc4 is present; detoxification reduces larval unpalatability to predators, so the detoxified state may reduce predator deterrence.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes detoxification of Lyc4 to RGHGL; BG1 silencing reduces RGHGL formation, increases Lyc4 accumulation, and causes moulting defects and mortality on Lyc4-containing diets.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step in Lyc4 detoxification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BG1 transcript is strongly induced in larval midgut by Lyc4 ingestion but not by RGHGL. 2. BG1 silencing by plant-mediated RNAi reduces Lyc4-to-RGHGL conversion in vitro and in vivo. 3. BG1-silenced larvae accumulate more Lyc4, excrete less RGHGL, and show moulting impairment and elevated mortality when feeding on Lyc4-containing plants. 4. Detoxification by BG1 decreases predator-deterrent Lyc4 levels, indicating detoxification rather than defensive co-option drives adaptation.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Candidate glycoside hydrolase sequences retrieved from NCBI; no study-wide deposited omics accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant chemical defence against herbivores; high Lyc4 levels also deter ingestion by predatory spiders
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BG1 performs unusual detoxification by partial deglycosylation rather than toxin activation; this contrasts with the common view that insect deglycosylation activates glycosylated phytotoxins. PMRi silencing was sequence-specific and did not affect BG2 or BG3.
